mentions.note: Venezuela oil trade already priced in at the open; upside from any production increase would be a drop in the bucket for Chevron regardless.
Good company, but oil leading the market is a problematic signal since energy costs act as a tax on the broader economy.
Throws off massive cash, big buyback, 4% yield. My favorite for very long time for consistency. Venezuela kicker as they operate there and know what needs doing.
Held since early 2000s Unocal deal, already up significantly; Cramer believes can go higher; strong 3.85% dividend; hold for continued energy demand upside.
Best-in-class assets under Michael Wirth with great yield and solid balance sheet; but stock has parabolic move — sell half or quarter Monday to lock gains despite company quality.
Substantial Berkshire position; geopolitical oil volatility limiting upside; Berkshire stock not participating in energy strength.
Oil stocks rallying on Middle East conflict expectations, but unclear if war is truly escalating; not a conviction call given policy contradictions.
More forward-looking than Exxon; Cramer's preferred E&P play for new buyers; Cramer regrets selling his own oil; recommends owning oil exposure (Chevron for new positions).
Worst performer in Dow today; oil -15% on ceasefire. Live by oil, die by oil. Leverage works both ways in energy sector.

Caller Sean asking about offshore drilling super cycle; Cramer says no, just go with Chevron (CEO Mike Worth drills offshore best); Caller Jim asks Exxon vs Chevron; Cramer sticks with Chevron—Worth runs better company.
Usually placid; listen closely to Iran war impact on transport plans; classic long-term approach but Venezuela easier than Middle East for oil extraction now.
Strong free cash flow, 3.8% dividend yield, solid CEO Mike Wirth; benefits from elevated oil environment amid Iran war. Caller sought entry; Cramer said 'Pull the trigger.'
